coredata.dc:title: Enhanced supercapacitor performance of Camellia oleifera shell derived hierarchical porous carbon by carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Quan, Hongying
Tao, Wenhao
Wang, Yan
Chen, Dezhi
coredata.dc:description: Biomass-derived porous carbons have great potential as electrode materials for supercapacitors. However, their amorphous and micropore-dominated structures are unfavorable for the electrochemical kinetics, which will limit their rate capability and cycle stability. In this work, we reported a facile strategy to improve the capacitive behavior of porous carbons derived from Camellia oleifera shell by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with highly graphitization degree into the skeleton of porous carbons. Benefiting from the unique surface property of CQDs, the obtained CQD/hierarchically porous carbon composites (CQDs/HPC) possessed a well-developed porous structure with high mesopore content and moderate surface heteroatom doping. Furthermore, their charge/ion transfer kinetics were improved due to the formation of the conductive channels in the carbon skeleton as well as optimized pore structure and surface doping. Consequently, the optimized CQDs/HPC-2 presented a high capacitance of 259 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a high rate capability in 1 M H2SO4, which is much better than bare HPC. The assembled symmetric supercapacitors delivered an energy density of 8.61 (17.86) Wh kg−1 at the power density of 477 (954) W kg−1 in 1 M H2SO4 (1 M Na2SO4) solution and high long-term stability with 94 % capacitance retention after 20,000 cycles at 5 A g−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Porous carbon
Carbon quantum dots
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical properties of aqueous symmetrical supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical SEM images of a–b) HPC, c–d) CQDs/HPC-1, e–f) CQDs/HPC-2, g–h) CQDs/HPC-3.
a) Typical TEM image and b) HRTEM image, SAED pattern (inset of b) and c) HRTEM of CQDs/HPC-2, d) element mapping of C, O and N, respectively.

a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms, b) pore-size distribution curves, c) histograms of Smicro and Smeso and the plot for the ratio of Smeso/Smicro, d) histograms of Vmicro and Vmeso and the plot for the ratio of Vmeso/Vmicro.
a) XRD patterns and b) Raman spectra of as-prepared CQDs/HPC.
XPS spectra of as-prepared CQDs/HPC. a) Survey scan, b) C 1s, c) N1s, d) O1s and e) atomic% of N and O species.

Capacitance properties of as-prepared HPC electrodes. a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1 of all HPC electrodes, b) CV of CQDs/HPC-2 at different scan rates, c) the fitted plots of the current density and the scan rate in charge processes of HPC electrodes, d) capacitive contributions of HPC electrodes at 10 mV s−1, e) capacitive contributions of CQDs/HPC-2 electrode at different scan rates, f) GCD-curves of HPC electrodes at 1 A g−1, g) GCD curves of CQDs/HPC-2 at different current densities, h) calculated specific capacitance of HPC electrodes at different current densities, i) EIS spectra of as-prepared HPC electrodes.
CV and GCD curves of a, b) CQDs/HPC-2 SSC and c, d) two CQDs/HPC-2 SSCs connected in series, e) Ragone plot of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC, f) long-term cycling performance of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC at 5 A g−1.
Schematic diagram for preparation process of CQDs/HPC composites.
Comparison of capacitance performance of biomass-derived porous carbon electrodes.

head.ce:title: Enhanced supercapacitor performance of Camellia oleifera shell derived hierarchical porous carbon by carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived porous carbons have great potential as electrode materials for supercapacitors. However, their amorphous and micropore-dominated structures are unfavorable for the electrochemical kinetics, which will limit their rate capability and cycle stability. In this work, we reported a facile strategy to improve the capacitive behavior of porous carbons derived from Camellia oleifera shell by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with highly graphitization degree into the skeleton of porous carbons. Benefiting from the unique surface property of CQDs, the obtained CQD/hierarchically porous carbon composites (CQDs/HPC) possessed a well-developed porous structure with high mesopore content and moderate surface heteroatom doping. Furthermore, their charge/ion transfer kinetics were improved due to the formation of the conductive channels in the carbon skeleton as well as optimized pore structure and surface doping. Consequently, the optimized CQDs/HPC-2 presented a high capacitance of 259 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a high rate capability in 1 M H2SO4, which is much better than bare HPC. The assembled symmetric supercapacitors delivered an energy density of 8.61 (17.86) Wh kg−1 at the power density of 477 (954) W kg−1 in 1 M H2SO4 (1 M Na2SO4) solution and high long-term stability with 94 % capacitance retention after 20,000 cycles at 5 A g−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Porous carbons are the most used electrode materials in electric double layer (EDL) supercapacitors (SCs) due to their high porosity, structural tunability and excellent chemical stability [1,2]. Generally, the carbonization and activation of recyclable biomass are considered as a cost-efficient approach to prepare porous carbons because of the advantages of eco-friendly resources and simple processing [3–5]. Up to now, numerous attempts have been devoted to the development of high activity porous carbons derived from biomass wastes via carbonization and activation processes [6,7]. According to previous reports, the porous carbons derived from biomass wastes through carbonization and activation treatments possess hierarchically porous structure with high specific surface area and excellent physicochemical stability, which are key factors of high capacitive performance electrode material for SCs. Besides, biomass-derived porous carbons generally contain abundant heteroatoms such as O, N, S and P, etc. [8–10] Therefore, biomass-derived porous carbons have been widely concerned as electrode materials in SCs. It is reported that the biomass precursors play an important role in the preparation of porous carbons materials for high-performance SC [2]. Camellia oleifera shell, byproducts of camellia oil, is one of the sustainable and freely assessable biomass sources contains cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, which is a promising precursor for producing porous carbon materials with high heteroatoms doping and porosity as well as excellent performance in energy storage and environmental remediation [11–13]. For example, Zhang et al. [14] prepared Camellia oleifera shell-derived porous carbon by ZnCl2 activation method, and the prepared porous carbon showed 374 and 266 F g−1 in 1 mol L−1 H2SO4 and 6 mol L−1 KOH electrolytes at 0.2 A g−1, respectively. Liang et al. [15] fabricated Camellia oleifera shell-derived porous carbon with high C/O ratio of 1.66 by convenient microwave assisted carbonization and KOH activation, and the porous carbon released high specific capacitance of 315 F g−1 in 2 mol L−1 H2SO4 and 251 F g−1 in 6 mol L−1 KOH at the current density of 0.5 A g−1. Bo et al. [16] developed a hierarchical carbon with oxygen-containing groups by microwave treatment from Camellia oleifera shell, and the hierarchical carbon delivered a high specific capacitance of 367 F g−1 in 2 mol L−1 H2SO4. However, the unsatisfied rate capability and long-term cycle stability still hampers the practical applications of Camellia oleifera shell-derived porous carbons in SC. Numerous works have been focused on tacking the limited energy density of porous carbons, including the further increase of specific surface areas and the tuning of pore structure as well as the increase of heteroatom doping [17–21]. However, the over-developed porous structure and the excessive heteroatom doping may do damage to the original conductive paths, thereby reducing the electric conductivity of porous carbon, resulting in sluggish electrochemical kinetics and low rate capability [22]. To resolve the above issues, high graphitized nanocarbons, such as graphene [23] or carbon nanotubes [24], had been introduced into biomass-derived porous carbons to improve their conductivity and obtain high capacitance performance. However, because of their large size, these conductive nanocarbons are challenging to enter the carbon skeleton of these porous carbons and cannot be combined with them at the molecular level to form an overall conductive channel. Therefore, the improvement of the conductivity of biomass-derived porous carbons is very limited.

Unlike graphene and carbon nanotubes, quasi-zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly crystallized carbon derivations [25]. In the recent past, CQDs have aroused considerable interest in the application for high-performance energy storage materials because of their excellent quantum confinement and edge effect [26,27]. The previous works suggested that CQDs modified on the surface of pseudocapacitor material, such as NiCo2O4 [28], LDH [27], etc., could improve their structural stability and electrochemical kinetics for electrochemical energy storage. In addition, owing to the edge effect and the rich doped heteroatoms of N, O, and P, etc., the modified CQDs could also provide extra active sites for energy storage [29,30].

Inspired by the above observation, herein, we developed a simple strategy to introduce highly crystallized CQDs into the Camellia oleifera shell-derived hierarchical porous carbon (HPC). Because of the edge effect and the heteroatom doping of CQDs, the as-prepared CQD/HPC composites showed much larger mesoporous specific surface area and porosity as well as more rational heteroatom doping than bare HPC. Furthermore, the constructed conductive channel by CQDs and the carbon skeleton of HPC could accelerate the electron transport and ion migration in CQD/HPC composites. According to the above advantages, the CQD/HPC composites delivered much better capacitance performances with a high specific capacitance of 259 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4 and excellent rate capability than that of bare HPC. Moreover, the assembled aqueous symmetric SCs using CQDs/HPC electrodes exhibited an energy density of 8.61 (17.86) Wh kg−1 at the power density of 477 (954) W kg−1 in 1 M H2SO4 (1 M Na2SO4) and excellent cycle stability with 94 % capacitance retention after 20,000 cycles at 5 A g−1, revealing a better practical application prospect in the energy storage device.

In a typical preparation process of CQDs/HPC composites (Scheme 1 ), 2 g of Camellia oleifera shell powder was dispersed in 120 mL of aqueous solution dissolving 8 g urea, which was used as nitrogen source and macropore-forming agent. A certain amount of CQDs solution (10, 15, 20 mL), which was prepared in Text S1 using the previous method [31], was injected under continuously stirred. The above mixture was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and carbonized at 180 °C for 12 h. After carbonization, the product was washed using deionized water and dried at 80 °C for 24 h. 1 g product powder and 2 g KOH were dispersed in 10 mL deionized water under continuously stirring for 12 h, and dried at 80 °C. Then, the obtained mixture was heated to 450 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 and maintained at 450 °C for 30 min under N2 atmosphere and continued to rise to 650 °C and held for 30 min, and finally raised to 800 °C and kept for 1 h. After activation, the cooled and obtained samples were immersed in 10 % HCl aqueous to remove inorganic impurities, then washed with deionized water until pH = 7, and dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The final product was denoted as CQDs/HPC-1, CQDs/HPC-2 and CQDs/HPC-3 representing the volume of the CQDs solution added was 10, 15, 20 mL for simplification in the rest of the manuscript, respectively. In addition, the bare HPC was prepared using the above process without CQDs.

The morphology and structure were observed on a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 250, FEI) and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X, FEI) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray detector (EDX). The specific surface area (SBET) and the pore size distribution were estimated using the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, obtained on a surface area and porosity analyzer (TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics) at 77 K. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and the t-plot models were used to calculate the specific surface area (SBET) and micropores area (Smicro), respectively. Non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) was adopted to analyze the size distribution. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on an X-ray powder diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at a scan rate of 5°/min. Raman spectra were obtained on a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR800, HORIBA Jobin Yvon) with a 632 nm excitation laser. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using the XPS spectrometer (Axis Ultra, Kratos).

The electrochemical properties of as-prepared samples were evaluated in 1 M H2SO4 using a three-electrode system. The working electrodes were prepared as follows. The as-prepared samples, acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride were uniformly dispersed in nitromethyl pyrrolidone with the mass ratio of 8:1:1 under continuous stirring for 24 h to form a homogeneous slurry. The obtained slurry was coated onto an inert carbon cloth (1 cm × 1.5 cm) and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The active materials loaded on carbon cloth were about 4 mg cm−2. Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum plate (1 cm × 1 cm) were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted on a CHI 760 E electrochemical workstation.

Besides, symmetrical supercapacitors (SCs), assembled by two as-prepared working electrodes, a polypropylene separator, and a few drops of 1 M H2SO4 or 1 M Na2SO4, were sealed in CR2016 type cell. CV and GCD curves of the SCs were obtained on a CHI760e electrochemical workstation, and the long-term cycle test was carried on a CT2001A supercapacitance test system.

The specific capacitance (C) was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
C =
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V
where I (A) represents the applied current, Δt (s) means the discharge time, m is the mass loading of active material in a single electrode and ΔV (V) is the potential window.

The energy density and power density of the SCs were calculated by the following Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively.
(2)
E =
1
2 × 3.6
C
Δ V
2
(3)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
where E (Wh kg−1) corresponds to the energy density, C (F g−1) is the specific capacitance of the symmetrical supercapacitors calculated using Eq. (1), ΔV (V) is the discharge voltage window, Δt (s) represents the discharge time, P (W kg−1) is the power density.

The morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared CQDs/HPC were characterized by SEM, and the obtained typical SEM images are shown in Fig. 1 . The bare HPC possessed a tremella-like structure (Fig. 1a), and there are no visible interconnected pore structures, but slight etching was formed on the surface of HPC (Fig. 1b). The surface of the HPC became rougher after the addition of 10 mL of CQDs, which showed a loose spongy-like structure (Fig. 1c), and pits are formed on the superficial parts of CQDs/HPC-1 (Fig. 1d). With the increase of CQDs volume to 15 mL, the surface of CQDs/HPC-2 became rougher and looser than CQDs/HPC-1. The pits on the surface are further etched, and numerous visible macropores of almost equal sizes were generated on the surface of CQDs/HPC-2 (Fig. 1e). Fig. 1f shows that the CQDs/HPC-2 presented a hierarchical porous structure with 3D interconnected channels and open pores. These connected and open pores can improve the accessibility of the active sites and provide short ion transport and accelerate electrolyte diffusion. As the volume of CQDs increased to 20 mL, the CQDs/HPC-3 showed a marshmallow-like structure (Fig. 1g). However, it can be observed that the hierarchical pore in CQDs/HPC-3 collapses and breaks (Fig. 1h). The SEM results indicate that the addition of CQDs has a substantial effect on the pore structure of HPC and is conducive to the formation of a hierarchical porous structure with 3D interconnected channels. Because of the presence of large hydrophilic groups on the edge of CQDs, it can serve as a surfactant to improve the carbonization and subsequent activation of biomass, resulting in the optimization of the pore structure of HPC. Clustering of CQDs will take place at the high CQDs dosage, and the locally excessive CQDs would cause the collapse of pore structure, which is disadvantageous to obtaining high electrochemical performance for HPC.

TEM was used further to observe the morphology and structure of the as-prepared materials. Fig. 2 displays the typical TEM images of CQDs/HPC-2. From Fig. 2a, some noticeable pores, including mesopores and macropores, can be observed the in 3D interconnected carbon framework of HPC. Additionally, the HRTEM image of Fig. 2b presents disorder lattice fringes, suggesting the amorphous characteristic of HPC and further confirmed by the corresponding SAED pattern shown in the inset of Fig. 2b. Furthermore, from the HRTEM images in Fig. 2c, crystallized domains (marked as circles) with a spacing of 0.24 nm corresponding to (002) planes of graphite carbon can be identified in the carbon skeleton of HPC, which confirms that highly crystallized CQDs (Fig. S1) were penetrated the structure of HPC and formed overall conductive channels inside the entire CQDs/HPC-2. The EDX mapping images (Fig. 2d) indicate that the elements of C, O, and N are uniformly dispersed in the CQDs/HPC-2.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were used to analyze the pore structure of as-prepared samples and shown in Fig. 3a. The quasi-type I isotherms with the sharp increase absorption at low-pressure region of P/P0 < 0.1 and the platform in the middle-pressure region of 0.2 < P/P0 < 0.95 indicate the dominant role of micropore. In addition, the H4 hysteresis loop in the middle-pressure region and the slightly increased adsorption in the high-pressure region (P/P0 > 0.95) demonstrate the presence of mesopores and macropores, respectively [32]. Fig. 3b displays the pore size (<50 nm) distribution curves of as-prepared samples. We can observe a microporous peak centered at around 0.9 nm and mesopores between 2 and 10 nm in all curves. Table S1 displays the detailed characteristic of pore structures. The HPC presents a SBET of 1495 m2 g−1 with a pore volume (Vtotal) of 0.834 cm3 g−1. The introducing CQDs can induce the increase of mesopore content in as-prepared composites. The CQDs/HPC-2 shows the highest specific surface area of 1750 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 0.981 cm3 g−1 in all samples. However, at the high CQDs dosage, the excessive CQDs will cause the collapse of mesopores, resulting in the decline of the mesopores in CQDs/HPC-3, which is consistent with the surface morphology analysis in Fig. 1g–h. The above results indicate the hierarchically porous structure of all samples. Previous reports demonstrated that the hierarchical pores are conducive to the formation of electrical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) when the pore size of porous carbon is larger than 0.8 nm and the mesoporous content of porous carbon is about 20–50 % [33,34], which is attributed to that mesopores can provide fast diffusion channels and store electrolyte as reservoirs for rapid ion diffusion and transfer [35,36]. To further express the role of CQDs in improving mesopores of composites, the histogram, and the ratio plot of Smeso/Smicro and Vmeso/Vmicro are displayed in Fig. 4c and d, respectively. The results show that the introduction of CQDs can increase the mesopores of as-prepared composites, and the CQDs/HPC-2 possessed the highest mesoporous proportion (37 %, Table S1) and in all samples. The large and well-developed porosity of CQDs/HPC-2 can offer more adsorption sites for ions and electrolyte infiltration to achieve fast ion transport and short diffusion, which is beneficial to enhancing the electrochemical performance [12]. According to these features, CQDs/HPC-2 should deliver an excellent electrochemical performance due to its high specific surface area and high mesoporous content.

Fig. 4a shows the XRD patterns of as-prepared samples. Two weak broad peaks around 26 and 43° are attributed to the (002) and (101) crystal plane of amorphous carbon. Compared with HPC, the (002) peaks of CQDs/HPC shift toward a low angle, indicating a low degree of crystallinity and an increase in the lattice spacing. It may be attributed to the presence of more pores and defects as the addition of CQDs. By comparison, CQDs/HPC-2 possessed the greatest lattice spacing due to the lowest diffraction angle (25.4°) of its (002) peak. The defect structure of as-prepared samples was further evaluated by Raman spectra shown in Fig. 4b. Two peaks centered at 1340 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 correspond to the D band of disordered carbon and G band of graphitized sp2 carbon, respectively. The degree of defect in as-prepared carbon materials can be expressed from the calculated intensity ratio of the D band and G band (ID/IG) [37]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the CQDs/HPC showed a higher ID/IG ratio than bare HPC, which agrees well with the XRD results, further confirming that more defects are introduced into the carbon framework with the addition of CQDs. The introduced defects could act as the active site for the electrochemical reactions and ion adsorption [34,38].

The chemical composition on the surface of the CQDs/HPC was characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 5a, XPS survey spectra of HPC and CQDs/HPC show three distinct characteristic peaks at around 284, 400, and 532 eV, which are assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The atomic percentage was calculated and listed in Table S2. The total atomic percentage of O plus N for all CQDs/HPC samples was higher than HPC and increased with the CQDs dosage. The high heteroatom doping of N and O is beneficial in improving the pseudocapacitance activities and wettability of the porous carbon [20,39,40]. Fig. 5b displays the high-resolution C 1s spectra, which can be divided into four species of CC, C-O/C-N, and CO located around 284.6, 285.6, and 288.5 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectra in Fig. 5c suggest that O species exist as OC (~531 eV), OC (~532.3 eV), COO (~533.6 eV), and adsorbed H2O and O2 (~534.8 eV), respectively. As shown in Table S2, the O content of CQDs/HPC (8.34 % for CQDs/HPC-1, 10.47 % for CQDs/HPC-2, 12.68 % for CQDs/HPC-3) was higher than HPC (7.75 %), suggesting that the addition of CQDs could increase the O content in as-prepared CQDs/HPC composites. Generally, the quinone-like CO and pyrone-like CO groups can provide pseudocapacitance, while COO can improve the wettability and improve the electrochemical properties for porous carbons [41]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. 5d can be fitted to three peaks around 398.3, 400.0 and 401.4 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic-N (N-6), pyrrolic N (N-5), and graphitized N (N-G), respectively [19]. The relative contents of different N species are collected in Table S2. The content of N species decreased slightly with the addition of CQDs.

The capacitive performances of CQDs/HPC in 1 M H2SO4 were measured using a three-electrode system. Fig. 6a shows the CV curves of the HPC and CQDs/HPC at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1, all CV curves display a rectangular-like shape with noticeable bumps, indicating that the capacitance of as-prepared porous carbons is dominated by EDLC behavior accompanied by pseudocapacitance stem from the doped N and O. Among all samples, the CQDs/HPC-2 exhibits the largest areas of the CV curves and the longest discharging times in the GCD curves. The detailed capacitive behaviors of CQDs/HPC-2 were further measured at different scan rates and current densities. The CV curves of the CQDs/HPC-2 still hold the original shapes even at 200 mV s−1, indicating the superior rate capability. To further explore the charge storage mechanism, the CV curves at variable scan rates from 10 to 200 mV s−1 were plotted. Generally, the peak current (i) in the CV curves obeys a power-law relationship with the sweep rate (ν), which is expressed as follows [42]:
(4)
i = a
v b
where a, b are variable parameters. The b value can be obtained by the straight fitting line of log i vs. log ν at various scan rates.
(5)
log
i
= b
log
v
+ log
a

As shown in Fig. 6c, the values of b for all-prepared electrodes are around 0.95 in the potential window of −1–0 V, suggesting that the surface-controlled capacitive mechanism dominates the charge storage in all as-prepared electrodes. The detailed capacitive contribution was further analyzed using Eqs. (6) and (7) [43,44]:
(6)
i
v
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v 0.5
(7)
i
v
v 0.5
=
k 1
v 0.5
+
k 2
where i is the current at a fixed potential V, k 1ν and k 2ν0.5 correspond to the capacitive contribution and diffusion-controlled processes, respectively. From Fig. 6d, the capacitive contribution for all as-prepared electrodes at 10 mV s−1 reaches as high as 72.9 %, and the capacitive contribution for CQDs/HPC-2 electrode increases with the increase of scan rate to 200 mV s−1 (Fig. 6e). Fig. 6f shows the GCD curves of all HPC electrodes at 1A g−1. The GCD profiles show a slight divergence from the typical triangular shape, suggesting the ideal capacitive behaviors of HPC electrodes. The longer discharging times of CQDs/HPC electrodes represent their higher specific capacitance than bare HPC electrode, indicating that the addition of CQDs has a positive effect on the improvement of capacitive performance of HPC. As shown in Fig. 6g, the GCD curves of the CQDs/HPC-2 remain symmetrical triangles even at the high current density of 20 A g−1, indicating the good reversible energy storage performance. According to the galvanostatic discharge curves shown in Figs. 6g and S2, the plots of calculated specific capacitance for all electrodes are shown in Fig. 6h. It indicates that the introduction of CQDs in HPC could increase the specific capacitance of as-prepared HPC electrodes. The optimized CQDs/HPC-2 exhibited the highest specific capacitance of 259 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 208.5 F g−1 even at 20 A g−1 with 80.5 % capacitance retention, which is much higher than that of bare HPC electrode and the recently reported biomass-derived porous carbon electrodes in Table 1 . This demonstrates that the CQDs are profitable to form uniformly distributed overall conductive channels in porous carbon skeleton, resulting in the improvement of the electrochemical performance of the CQDs/HPC, especially at high current densities. The electrochemical kinetics of electrodes were further analyzed using EIS. Fig. 6i shows the Nyquist plots of all electrodes in the frequency range from 10,000 to 0.01 Hz with an alternate current amplitude of 5 mV at open circuit potential. The intercept from the abscissa represents the internal resistance (R
s ) of the electrode. In the high-frequency region, the diameter of the quasi-semicircle corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) between the electrode and the electrolyte [45]. Table S3 shows the fitted value of R
ct and R
s . The values of R
ct for CQDs/HPC electrodes are lower than the bare HPC, and the CQDs/HPC-2 show the lowest R
ct , suggesting that the CQDs uniformly embedding in carbon skeleton could accelerate the charge transfer. A nearly vertical line in the low-frequency region demonstrates the presence of fast ion diffusion in as-prepared electrode materials [46].

The good electrochemical performance of CQDs/HPC is mainly due to the synergistic effect between CQDs and HPC (Fig. S3). First, the advantages of abundant functional groups and edge sites of CQDs can improve the porous structure and heteroatom doping of the HPC. Second, the homogeneously distributed CQDs with highly crystalline in the amorphous carbon skeleton can significantly promote the electron transport in the whole CQDs/HPC electrodes, which is conducive to the migration and storage of ion in deep and branched pores. This can improve the utilization of the large surface area and enhance the electrochemical kinetics and rate performance. According to the above discussion, CQDs/HPC-2 has a high specific surface area, optimal pore structure, and suitable N, O containing functional groups, ensuring sufficient and short ion transport paths, resulting in much enhanced capacitive performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of aqueous symmetrical supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the application potential of CQDs/HPC electrodes, aqueous symmetrical SCs were assembled using two identical CQDs/HPC-2 electrodes, denoted as CQDs/HPC-2 SSC. Fig. 7a and b display the CV and GCD curves of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC with the voltage window of 0–1 V in 1 M H2SO4, respectively. The quasi-rectangular shape of CV curves and quasi-symmetric triangle of GCD curves indicate a good reversible energy storage behavior. Based on the discharge curves in Fig. 7b, the calculated specific capacitance of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC was 68.0 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1. It remained 53.8 F g−1 with capacitance retention of 79.1 % at 10 A g−1. However, the deformation in the CV curves and inconspicuous voltage platform over 0.8 V in the charge curves suggest that the voltage window of 0–0.8 V is appropriate for the CQDs/HPC-2 SSC. Therefore, two CQDs/HPC-2 SSCs were connected in series. Fig. 7c and d show the obtained CV and GCD curves of two CQDs/HPC-2 SSCs with the voltage window of 0–1.6 V in 1 M H2SO4, respectively. The rectangular shape of CV curves and symmetric triangle of GCD curves demonstrate an ideal capacitive behavior of CQDs/HPC-2 SSCs. According to the discharge curves shown in Fig. 7b and d, the calculated energy density (E) of the CQDs/HPC-2 SSC was 8.61 Wh kg−1, and the corresponding power density (P) was 477 W kg−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1. When the current density increased to 10 A g−1, the calculated E for one and two CQDs/HPC-2 SSCs connected in series was 2.67 and 2.80 Wh kg−1, and the corresponding P was 3014 and 5934 W kg−1, respectively. As shown in the Fig. 7e and Table 1, the energy storage performance of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC is better than the recently reported SSCs assembled by electrodes of porous carbon derived from numerous biomasses, including cucumber, tobacco waste, jatropha oil cake, trees, Parkia javanica seedpod, kelp, soybean straw, mung bean jelly, pinecone, leaves, bamboo fungus, peel, etc. To further improve the energy density of the assemble CQDs/HPC-2 SSC, 1 M Na2SO4 was used as electrolyte since it owns large operating voltage, and the obtained capacitive properties are displayed in Fig. S4. We can see that the operating voltage window of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC can be extended to 2 V. Under the current density of 1 A g−1, the CQDs/HPC-2 SSC released an energy density of 17.86 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 954 W kg−1 and remained an energy density of 6.01 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 6363 W kg−1 under the current density of 10 A g−1 in 1 M Na2SO4 solution. Fig. 7g shows the long-term cycling stability of CQDs/HPC-2 SSC in 1 M H2SO4. The capacitance retention of 94 % was achieved after 20,000 GCD cycles at 5 A g−1, and the Coulombic efficiency was about 100 %.

Herein, the highly crystallized CQDs were used to construct overall conductive channels inside the porous carbon skeleton derived from Camellia oleifera shell. The obtained CQDs/HPC possesses a high specific surface area, optimal pore structure, and suitable N, O containing functional groups, which is beneficial to increase the active sites and improve the charge transfer kinetics and ion transfer rate. The obtained CQDs/HPC-2 showed a high capacitance of 259 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and excellent rate performance in 1 M H2SO4. Furthermore, the symmetric supercapacitor assembled by CQDs/HPC-2 delivered an energy density of 8.61 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 477 W kg−1 in 1 M H2SO4 and 17.86 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 954 W kg−1 in 1 M Na2SO4 solution. Our work indicates that the uniform distribution of highly crystallized GQDs in carbon skeleton shows excellent potential for improving the energy storage performance of biomass-derived porous carbons. This strategy not only provides a new idea for the design of high-performance electrode materials, but also offers a new solution for the efficient utilization of biomass waste.
Hongying Quan: Experimental design, Data curation, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – reviewing and editing, Resources, Funding Acquisition.
Wenhao Tao: Investigation, Data curation.
Yan Wang: Investigation, Data curation.
Dezhi Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – Review & Editing, Project Administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52063023, 51968049), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province, China (Grant No. 20202ACBL204012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2022.105573.
